evidence: The contract relationship between upload runtime behavior and protocol catalog is enforced via tests (`TestUploadParserActionsMatchUploadContractRegistry`, `TestUploadActionBindingsValidateRuntimePayloadRules`, and conformance fixture parity tests) rather than by constructing upload action behavior directly from a single shared registry at runtime.

Contract ownership metadata is modeled differently across sibling contract catalogs: `action/specs.go` defines a typed `OwnerModule` enum-like type, while `startram/specs.go` uses an untyped string constant (`OwnerStartram`), weakening uniform boundary contracts across modules.
No import-time side effects were found in the assigned files; all files are constant/spec/test declarations and functions without module-scope runtime execution.

Large callback-bag runtimes are repeated across modules: `importerRuntime` (storage/upload/provision groups) in `goseg/importer/importer.go`, `workflowRuntime` in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`, `startupInitRuntime`/`StartupRuntime` in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`, and `Runtime*Ops` families in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`.

Broadcast lifecycle code stacks thin wrappers with near-identical behavior (`StartBroadcastLoop`, `StartBroadcastLoopWithRuntime`, `newBroadcastLoopRuntimeWithDefault`; plus `bootstrapBroadcastState` and `bootstrapBroadcastStateWithRuntime`) across `goseg/broadcast/loop.go` and `goseg/broadcast/state_lifecycle.go`.

Global runtime setters/getters (`DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime`, `SetDefaultBroadcastStateRuntime`, `SetDefaultEventRuntime`) encourage bypassing explicit dependencies and dilute abstraction boundaries (`goseg/broadcast/state.go`, `goseg/docker/events/events.go`).

Core flows bind to mutable package-level singletons (`defaultEventRuntime`, `defaultBroadcastStateRuntime`, `defaultDockerSubsystemRuntime`) and are then consumed by other subsystems as implicit dependencies (`goseg/docker/events/events.go`, `goseg/broadcast/state.go`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go`).

Default runtime construction wires broad cross-package dependencies directly (`newWorkflowRuntime` pulls docker orchestration runtime + event runtime + click + net resolver in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`; `defaultImporterRuntime` also pulls docker orchestration and event runtime in `goseg/importer/importer.go`).
This pattern increases transitive coupling and makes dependency direction harder to reason about, even without explicit Go import cycles.
All five files are package-level `doc.go` comments with concise domain-oriented rationale rather than repetitive line-by-line restatements.
No deprecated markers, migration TODO stubs, or placeholder text were found in the assigned files.
`goseg/uploadsvc/doc.go` includes a concrete governance reference (`docs/architecture/contracts-governance.md`) instead of generic boilerplate.
ai_generated_debt.issues_preventing_higher_score: Several docs make strong behavioral guarantees (ordering, normalization, privileged sequencing) without consistently linking to contract artifacts or migration notes, which increases future drift risk and resembles polished-but-unanchored prose.

evidence: holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles shows very large flat directories around these files: goseg/shipworkflow/ (55 files), goseg/config/ (41 files), and goseg/docker/orchestration/ (34 files), each above the >10 mixed-concern threshold in the rubric.

goseg/shipworkflow/newship_service.go combines transport action routing, key decoding, drive resolution, async orchestration kick-off, and patp normalization/validation tables in one package/file boundary.

goseg/config/bootstrap.go and goseg/config/config.go span bootstrapping, global runtime state, context facade wrappers, and settings snapshots, indicating ownership boundaries are split across concerns but kept in one root package.

goseg/docker/orchestration/container_bridge.go and container_config.go are adapter/build-composition files that primarily wire subcomponents (container runtimes/config builders) yet live in the same broad orchestration root package.

`goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go` validates action fixtures and upload payload-rule invariants, but does not exercise StarTram contract catalog invariants from `goseg/protocol/contracts/catalog/startram/specs.go`.

`goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go` validates upload action parsing and payload-rule enforcement, but test coverage remains action-family-centric and does not provide cross-family governance checks promised by contract governance language in `README.md`.

Runtime seam layers rely on repeated hand-written delegator methods with per-method nil guarding in both `goseg/docker/events/events.go` (`DelegatingEventBroker` publish/read methods) and `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_core.go` (`StartupRuntime` and `Runtime` wrapper methods).

Large test modules repeatedly build bespoke runtime/config fixtures instead of reusing shared builders: `goseg/config/config_test.go` has long inline `defaultCfg/customCfg` field-by-field setup, while `goseg/importer/importer_test.go`, `goseg/main_test.go`, and `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator_test.go` each define local runtime-option DSLs.

Global seam/state reset patterns are repeated heavily in tests (`goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go` repeated reset helpers, `goseg/system/wifi_test.go` repeated `resetWifiSeamsForTest` + runtime construction), indicating mixed test concerns (state hygiene + behavior specification) in single files.

goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go defines very large public seam types (e.g., RuntimeContainerOps, RuntimeUrbitOps, RuntimeSnapshotOps) that expose many unrelated callbacks as one API surface.
goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go embeds multiple large seam structs directly into workflowRuntime, so callers inherit a broad, mixed API rather than a focused contract.

goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go contains paired APIs with near-duplicate behavior (RunTransitionedOperation vs RunTransitionedOperationWithRuntime), increasing surface area without a clear boundary.

Error-message casing/style is inconsistent across sibling runtime code: lowercase wrapped errors in goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go (e.g., "prepare wifi runtime..."), but title-case/legacy phrasing in goseg/shipworkflow/newship_service.go (e.g., "Couldn't...", "No new ship...").
goseg/docker/orchestration/container_config.go uses title-cased error text ("Unrecognized container type") while most other packages return lowercase error messages.

The same subsystem family uses mixed naming conventions for callback fields (e.g., GetWgConfFn, LoadMCFn, SvcDeleteFn in goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go), creating a style island of abbreviations.
goseg/shipworkflow/newship_service.go publishes transitions via events.DefaultEventRuntime().PublishNewShipTransition but discards returned errors in multiple places (HandleNewShipBoot/ResetNewShip).

goseg/broadcast/transitions.go and goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go explicitly route publish failures through transition.HandleTransitionPublishError, indicating a different (stricter) policy in related flows.
This produces mixed failure contracts for transition publishing: some paths are fail-aware and policy-driven, others silently ignore publish failures.
goseg/main.go uses package init() to derive web filesystem state and assign process-wide globals (webContent/fileServer/initWebErr), creating import-time initialization behavior.
goseg/config/config_update_schema.go uses init() to build and validate global patch registries (configPatchByKey/configPatchByKeyErr), making correctness depend on package-load side effects.

Several modules depend on mutable process-wide defaults (e.g., goseg/broadcast/state.go DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime, goseg/docker/events/events.go DefaultEventRuntime, goseg/config/bootstrap.go package globals/initOnce), which couples startup/order and test isolation.

goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go contains opaque and abbreviation-heavy API names (e.g., LoadMCFn, SvcDeleteFn, UrbitConfFn, GetLusCodeFn) mixed with fully spelled names, reducing intent clarity.

goseg/shipworkflow/newship_service.go exposes legacy-styled names/messages (e.g., "NewShip" tokening and "Unknown NewShip action") that diverge from clearer action/catalog terminology used in protocol contract files.
goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go repeatedly uses generic terms like runtime/ops/coordinator for distinct responsibilities, making boundaries harder to infer from names alone.

findings.summary: Cross-module contract consistency is guarded primarily by tests instead of a single runtime source-of-truth API.
evidence: `actions_test.go` builds expectations from `contracts.UploadActionBindingSpecs()` and verifies uploadsvc mappings/parsers match, indicating duplicate representations are synchronized by tests.
`fixtures_test.go` separately validates fixture-to-binding parity (`len(fixtures) == len(bindings)` and key-based matching), reinforcing a pattern of cross-package consistency checks in tests.
`action/specs.go` still defines explicit action catalog entries separately, so architecture currently depends on parity checks rather than one shared consumed registry.
findings.suggestion: Expose one canonical action-binding registry API from contracts and make uploadsvc parser/action-contract resolution derive directly from it at runtime; keep tests as regression checks rather than primary synchronization mechanism.

findings.summary: Owner metadata uses inconsistent modeling across catalog packages, weakening boundary governance semantics.
evidence: `action/specs.go` introduces `type OwnerModule string` with named constants (`OwnerUploadService`, `OwnerSystemWiFi`).
`startram/specs.go` declares owner as plain string constant (`OwnerStartram = "startram-domain"`) without shared typed model.
`uploadsvc/doc.go` references cross-package governance policy, but owner typing is not consistently enforced in the contract catalog implementation.
findings.suggestion: Create a shared owner-domain type (and canonical constants) in a common contracts package and require all catalog specs to use it, so ownership boundaries are validated consistently across modules.

findings.summary: Runtime seam structs have grown into low-cohesion callback bags with high indirection cost.
evidence: `importerRuntime` embeds three large runtime groups and many callback fields, then validates by tag groups rather than domain-focused interfaces.
`workflowRuntime` embeds multiple `dockerOrchestration.Runtime*Ops` bundles plus local callbacks; behavior is largely forwarding orchestration dependencies.
`startupInitRuntime`/`StartupRuntime` carry many function fields and policy wrappers, creating setup-heavy indirection for straightforward startup flows.
findings.suggestion: Split runtime bags into smaller capability interfaces per use-case (for example: transition publisher, container lifecycle, config patching, network checks) and pass only required capabilities into each operation. Remove tag-group validation as the primary composition mechanism and replace with constructor-level typed dependencies.

findings.summary: Broadcast state/loop APIs duplicate wrapper layers that add little policy value.
evidence: `StartBroadcastLoop`, `StartBroadcastLoopWithRuntime`, `newBroadcastLoopRuntimeWithDefault`, and `newBroadcastLoopRuntimeWithController` form a wrapper chain where many functions only normalize runtime/controller arguments.
`bootstrapBroadcastState` delegates directly to `bootstrapBroadcastStateWithRuntime` with repeated nil checks and similar error text.
Shared mutable default runtime/controller globals are introduced alongside wrapper entrypoints, increasing API surface and call-path depth.
findings.suggestion: Collapse broadcast entrypoints to one explicit runtime-based API and one minimal compatibility shim; move default singleton resolution to composition/bootstrap layer. Keep lifecycle functions focused on one responsibility (bootstrap vs loop control) and remove duplicated nil-guard wrappers.

findings.summary: Mutable package-level default runtimes create implicit cross-subsystem dependencies.
evidence: `defaultEventRuntime` and `defaultBroadcastStateRuntime` are global mutable dependencies exposed via `SetDefault*` and `Default*` accessors.
Subsystem and workflow code retrieves defaults directly (`events.DefaultEventRuntime()`, default broadcast runtime paths) instead of receiving brokers/runtimes at boundaries.
Multiple modules then build their own default runtime bundles that depend on these globals, making dependency flow implicit and order-sensitive.
findings.suggestion: Adopt explicit dependency injection at subsystem boundaries: create immutable runtime objects at startup, pass `EventBroker`/broadcast runtime/context interfaces explicitly, and deprecate package-level `SetDefault*` mutators except in legacy compatibility adapters.

findings.summary: New-ship transport handling, provisioning orchestration, and patp validation logic are co-located in the same top-level package boundary.
evidence: directory_profiles lists goseg/shipworkflow/ with 55 files, indicating a flat high-cardinality package.
newship_service.go includes HandleNewShip action dispatch plus low-level normalizePatp/isValidPatp implementation and lexical tables, while also invoking ProvisionShip orchestration paths.
newship.go builds provisioning runtime wiring across config/docker/network/shipcreator concerns, keeping composition logic in the same root package as request/validation logic.
findings.suggestion: Stage 1: create `goseg/shipworkflow/newship/` and move action handlers (`HandleNewShip`, `HandleNewShipBoot`, reset/cancel) there. Stage 2: move patp normalization/validation into `goseg/shipworkflow/validation/` (or reuse an existing shared validator package if present), then replace direct calls with that package API. Stage 3: keep runtime wiring in `goseg/shipworkflow/provisioning/` and expose a thin orchestrator entrypoint in root. Stage 4: run `goimports -w goseg/shipworkflow/**/*.go`, then `go test ./goseg/shipworkflow/...` and `go test ./...` to verify import updates and behavior.

findings.summary: The root config package mixes bootstrap lifecycle, mutable process state, and context facade APIs instead of separating ownership layers.
evidence: directory_profiles lists goseg/config/ with 41 files, well above the mixed-concern split threshold.
bootstrap.go performs startup sequencing and state initialization (`Initialize`, path/debug setup, persistence interaction, global Ready/state toggles).
config.go mainly delegates to `config/contextapi` and `config/startramstate` while remaining in root, showing boundary inversion where facade and lifecycle logic share the same package surface.
findings.suggestion: Stage 1: create `goseg/config/bootstrap/` for initialization workflow and `goseg/config/runtime/` for process-context facade/state APIs. Stage 2: move `Initialize` flow and boot helpers into `bootstrap`, preserving stable exported wrappers in `config` temporarily. Stage 3: migrate context facade wrappers and snapshot accessors into `runtime`, then narrow root `config` to compatibility exports. Stage 4: run `goimports -w goseg/config/**/*.go`, then `go test ./goseg/config/...` and `go test ./...` to validate import rewrites and package wiring.

findings.summary: Container runtime/config adapter composition files are placed in the broad orchestration root package instead of a dedicated adapter/configbuilder boundary.
evidence: directory_profiles lists goseg/docker/orchestration/ with 34 files while also having focused subfolders (`container/`, `internal/`, `subsystem/`), indicating partial but incomplete decomposition.
container_bridge.go is a wiring-heavy adapter (large callback-input struct + runtime merge templates) whose responsibility is composition, not orchestration behavior.
container_config.go is a typed dispatch map over container types plus runtime-backed builder selection, another composition concern currently mixed into the root orchestration namespace.
findings.suggestion: Stage 1: create `goseg/docker/orchestration/adapters/` (runtime bridges) and `goseg/docker/orchestration/configbuilder/` (type-to-config dispatch). Stage 2: move `container_bridge.go` into adapters and `container_config.go` into configbuilder; keep forwarding functions in root for backward compatibility. Stage 3: update callers (including shipworkflow provisioning paths) to import the new packages directly. Stage 4: run `goimports -w goseg/docker/orchestration/**/*.go goseg/shipworkflow/**/*.go`, then `go test ./goseg/docker/orchestration/...` and `go test ./...` to validate import/path and behavior stability.

findings.summary: Owner-domain contracts use inconsistent types across action and startram catalogs.
evidence: `action/specs.go` introduces `type OwnerModule string` and uses `string(OwnerUploadService)` / `string(OwnerSystemWiFi)`.
`startram/specs.go` uses raw string `OwnerStartram` with no shared typed taxonomy.
Conformance tests in `fixtures_test.go` do not assert owner taxonomy validity across catalogs.
findings.suggestion: Create a shared owner-domain type and allowed-value registry in a common contracts package (for example under `goseg/protocol/contracts/familyspec` or `familycatalog`), migrate both catalogs to that shared type, and add a conformance test that iterates all catalog specs and fails on unknown owner values.

findings.summary: Contract conformance tests skip StarTram catalog invariants covered by governance scope.
evidence: `fixtures_test.go` includes action fixture matching, unknown-action rejection, namespace validation, and upload payload-rule checks, but no assertions over `startram.ContractSpecs()`.
`startram/specs.go` defines externally meaningful contract metadata (ID/name/message/owner) without a corresponding conformance assertion in this batch scope.
`README.md` describes a contract conformance gate and governance policy, but current tests in scope mostly validate upload/action paths.
findings.suggestion: Add StarTram-focused conformance tests that validate catalog IDs, ownership, descriptor/message invariants, and uniqueness against other contract families; include these in the same governance gate path used for `./protocol/contracts/...` so action and StarTram families are both enforced by CI.

findings.summary: Runtime/delegate APIs are implemented via repetitive hand-written forwarding patterns, increasing structural drift risk.
evidence: `DelegatingEventBroker` re-implements near-identical nil-check + forward logic for each event type method in `events.go`.
`Runtime`/`StartupRuntime` in `runtime_core.go` repeat many `if fn == nil { default/error } else forward` methods.
`runtime_ops.go` defines a very wide callback-surface with many function-pointer fields, which amplifies the amount of boilerplate adapters needed in surrounding runtime wrappers.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a smaller set of cohesive capability interfaces (for example grouped by event stream or startup phase) and centralize nil/default handling in shared helper adapters so per-method forwarding code is generated or reduced to table-driven registration.

findings.summary: Test runtime/config fixture construction is duplicated across modules instead of using shared composable builders.
evidence: `config_test.go` repeatedly sets dozens of `defaultCfg`/`customCfg` fields inline across multiple tests, embedding fixture assembly into each scenario.
`importer_test.go` defines custom runtime helpers (`importerRuntimeWith`, `authorizeTokenIDsForTest`, `init/upload/provisionRuntime`) that duplicate builder responsibilities seen elsewhere.

`main_test.go` (`c2cTestRuntime`/`withC2CState`) and `startup_orchestrator_test.go` (`withStartBackgroundServicesRuntime`/callback options) each create separate ad-hoc option DSLs for similar seam wiring.
findings.suggestion: Extract shared test-fixture packages for common config/runtime seams (e.g., `internal/testfixtures/config` and `internal/testfixtures/runtime`) with preset builders and scenario overlays; keep per-test files focused on assertions rather than object graph assembly.

findings.summary: Several test files mix global-state lifecycle management with behavior specs, creating multi-responsibility test modules.
evidence: `accesspoint_test.go` contains many tests that repeatedly invoke global reset helpers (`resetRouterGlobalsForTest` / parameter resets), coupling scenario logic to mutable global seam maintenance.
`wifi_test.go` repeatedly calls `resetWifiSeamsForTest` and redefines runtime seams per test; the reset helper itself is empty, indicating state/reset behavior is not consistently encapsulated.
`importer_test.go` carries upload-session global map reset/copy helpers and event draining helpers in the same file as action-level behavioral assertions.
findings.suggestion: Move mutable global seam/session lifecycle into dedicated test harness utilities with explicit setup/teardown contracts, and keep behavior tests table-driven over immutable fixture inputs.

findings.summary: Runtime seam APIs are oversized callback bags that mix unrelated concerns.
evidence: RuntimeContainerOps/RuntimeUrbitOps/RuntimeSnapshotOps in runtime_ops.go expose many unrelated operations in single public structs.
workflowRuntime in operations_runtime.go embeds these large structs directly, broadening every consumer contract.
StartupRuntime in startup_orchestrator.go follows a similar callback-bag pattern, reinforcing the same API shape across subsystems.
findings.suggestion: Split seam structs by bounded capability (for example: transition publishing, container lifecycle, ship config persistence) and inject only the minimal interface per workflow; deprecate broad bag structs behind adapter constructors.

findings.summary: Error text conventions drift across adjacent modules (case, phrasing, abbreviations).
evidence: newship_service.go returns title-case legacy strings like "Couldn't unmarshal..." and "No new ship...".
wifi_runtime_api.go predominantly uses lowercase wrapped errors like "prepare wifi runtime...".
container_config.go returns "Unrecognized container type..." (title-case), differing from lower-case error style used elsewhere.
findings.suggestion: Define and enforce an error text convention (lowercase, action-first, wrapped context) and normalize strings in these modules to one style; add tests for stable error text where API contracts depend on it.

findings.summary: New-ship transition publishing ignores publish errors while sibling flows enforce policy.
evidence: HandleNewShipBoot and ResetNewShip call PublishNewShipTransition without checking returned errors.
broadcast/transitions.go explicitly handles publish failures via transition.HandleTransitionPublishError.
operations_runtime.go also funnels publish errors through transition policy handling.
findings.suggestion: Route new-ship transition publication through the same policy-handled helper used by other transition paths, and propagate or classify publish failures instead of dropping them.

findings.summary: Import-time init and mutable process-wide defaults create boot-order coupling.
evidence: main.go init() eagerly prepares web FS globals at package load.
config_update_schema.go init() builds/validates global registry state at import time.
broadcast/state.go and docker/events/events.go rely on mutable default runtime singletons, and config/bootstrap.go maintains package-global mutable process state guarded by initOnce/mutexes.
findings.suggestion: Move init-time work behind explicit bootstrap constructors, pass runtime instances through startup wiring, and treat package-level defaults as temporary adapters for legacy call sites only.

findings.summary: Abbreviation-heavy seam names reduce API intent clarity and consistency.
evidence: runtime_ops.go mixes terse abbreviations (LoadMCFn, SvcDeleteFn, UrbitConfFn, GetLusCodeFn) with fully spelled names in the same API surface.
operations_runtime.go relies on broad generic names (runtime, coordinator, ops) across multiple responsibilities.
newship_service.go uses "NewShip" naming and literal action labels that are less descriptive than the contract-oriented naming used in protocol catalog modules.
findings.suggestion: Standardize seam naming to explicit verb+noun patterns (for example, LoadMinIOClientConfig, DeleteServiceByPatpKind, LoadUrbitConfig) and align shipworkflow action naming with protocol contract vocabulary.
